Guardian of Flame

Phaedra orbits a pair of bright orange suns, incandescent hammers that pound down on its dusty surface. A ring of rock and shattered steel encircles the planet, perhaps the stillborn remains of a failed moon now slowly sucking up the wreckage of your predecessors. You drift [pc_ship.theName] towards the planet, nice and slow, putting every watt of spare power into shields and sensors. And it’s a good thing you do. Just as you’re nearing orbit, your sensors register a Confederate ident tag: a KihaCorp corvette, the Guardian of Flame, rising out of Phaedra’s ring. Compared to [pc_ship.theName], the thing’s massive, military sized, and though she seems to be in perfect fighting condition, your scan doesn’t pick up any life signs. Automated? Would make sense for the galaxy’s most renowned A.I. firm. The only lights you can see on visual are the twin red glows of its engines, pinpricks of crimson against the slanted black panes of its hull and the dark vastness of space beyond. As you’re reading the scan, a streak of red text flashes over your screen: ALERT: MISSILE LOCK. The Guardian’s engines flare. The long, sleek corvette comes about to face you with alarming speed, energy signatures building around its launchers and main cannon.
“Boss... I think we just found what killed all those other ships.”
You can fight or flee, but that drone ship’s between you and the planet below - between you and the next probe!

While you’re still catching your breath, another contact appears on your sensors from the middle of the debris field, its engines and shields flaring to life from silent running. MaxCorp idents flick onto your screen, followed closely by a radio call with a familiar, familial voice:

“Thanks for clearing the way, loser!” your cousin laughs, blasting past you towards the surface. “And while you were busy fighting, my passive scans picked up where the probe is! Hahaha, I’m a damn genius.”\n\nFucking hell [rival.hisHer] ship is fast for such a little yacht. You grab the controls and kick the engines up, chasing after [rival.himHer].
Damn it, after all this and [rival.heShe]’s still trying to be such a pain in your ass...

Well, if your cousin’s dumb enough to show your [rival.hisHer] ass, you’re happy to start calculating a target lock. Pipsqueak ship that zippy must have traded all its armor for engines, so it’ll be easy pickings.

[rival.HisHer] ship scrapes the upper atmosphere, angled towards one of the few verdant parts left on the hellworld down below: a green and blue forest surrounded by a ring of mountains, a jade oasis in the vast desert surrounding it.

[rival.name]’s shields suddenly flare, crackling white-hot as [rival.heShe] enters the atmosphere - that’s not just re-entry heat. You pull the stick to the side, breaking off your own chase just a split second before your cousin’s shields collapse, and several armor plates buckle under impact. “Oh, no!” [rival.heShe] screams, and then the comm line goes dead. Black smoke streaks from the stricken ship’s engines, and you can see flames billowing from aft compartments. [rival.name] keeps taking hits from the surface until [rival.heShe] has no choice but to veer off course, arcing away from the oasis and into the desert - deep into one of the zones your own sensors had flagged as dangerously radioactive.
You hit the comms, shouting “[rival.name]! Are you okay?” over the open waves. Silence. “Shit!”
No way you can land down there. [rival.name]’s going to be lucky if [rival.heShe] doesn’t grow a third arm or something down there; even your microsurgeons aren’t a guarantee of safety.

“That’s for the sabotage on Tavros!” you laugh, watching your cousin’s ship crash into the dirt. Too bad you can’t set down nearby to finish things, but [rival.name]’s going to be lucky if [rival.heShe] doesn’t grow a third arm or something down there; even your microsurgeons aren’t a guarantee of safety.
With a heavy sigh, you scan for the best landing site outside the red zone and head for it.
Next

As if the Kiha corvette wasn’t bad enough, now there’s anti-aircraft cannons surrounding where your cousin thought the damn probe was. This planet really is hostile[silly| to life as you know it]. Even the LZ you pick out has low-yield radiological alerts - though this is within the margin of safety provided by your microsurgeons at least.

The ground outside is dusty and dry, windswept thanks to the lack of vegetation to break it; there’s only rock and low-clinging grass as far as the eye can see. Well, that and sand. So, so much sand. You heave up from the captain’s seat and collect your kit. At the very least, if [rival.name] survived that crash, you’ve got plenty of time to catch up to [rival.hisHer] lead.
Kihan Cultist

There’s a cloaked figure standing next to your ship, carrying a brass-gilded multitool of such stupendous height compared to its owner that it nearly dwarfs her. She’s busily clanging the thing against your hull, stopping after every strike to make arcane gestures with her left hand and consult an old-school LCD screen affixed to her right wrist. A pair of small, frilled ears peek out from beneath her hood, twitching at the resonance of each blow to [pc_ship.theName]’s armor.

That hooded cloak is also just about all she’s wearing besides some linen wraps around her hands and feet, giving you a good look at everything the strange techno-cultist has on offer: she’s about five and a half feet tall, with pale green... not skin, but rather full body scales that cover her from her clawed toes to the tiny hint of a snout cresting her face, fringed by ash-colored hair. The woman’s incredibly voluptuous, with tremendously broad hips that sit well past her shoulders, pendulous breasts each capped with a blooming crimson flower where a human would have a nipple, and... well, that’s certainly something hanging between her legs, but you honestly can’t be sure it’s a cock per se. It looks like a pinecone but made of flower petals, and it’s positively soaked through with moisture that trickles up from a juicy stalk in its center and out between its myriad lush petals. A long, thick tail sprouts from above her plump behind, swishing to and fro excitedly as she continues to crack her tool-staff against the side of your ship.

You [pc.nmh|clear your throat|whistle|crack your [pc.weapon] against the airlock] to get her attention, giving the cloaked woman such a start that she nearly drops her tool. She yelps, fumbling to catch it before it drops, and then turns to you with a lopsided grin full of triangular teeth.
“Your airship is magnificent!” she says, running a hand through her ashen hair. Several strands of it wrap themselves around her wrist, trailing after her as if with a will of their own. “
In good condition, too. We haven’t been getting many of these through since the First Skyfall.
A bit beat up, but I’ve seen a lot worse since the First Skyfall. A loooot worse!
Oh, it’s a shame about all the damage, but that’s the same with basically every ship coming through since First Skyfall!

“First what?” you echo back. “"+({var0}.isAss()?"And why are you hitting my ship if you like it so much?":"Stop hitting my ship!")+"”\n\nShe glances between you and the hull, biting her azure lip. “Sorry, I couldn’t help myself. original: Kihan Cultist
Cultist Selfies
“Sure, I guess,” you say, hopping down the ramp and posing in front of [pc_ship.theName].

She fiddles with the computer on her wrist, tail wagging hard enough to kick up a sandcloud behind her. “Perfect! I will tell all my sisters that Emberians are very friendly! Much friendlier than the bunch of xenos that dropped on First Skyfall. Whew, they were real pieces of work...”

Might as well take the opportunity to work her for some information, right? “What’s First Skyfall, anyway? Your planet’s first contact with aliens?” “Uh-huh! Was about, oh, six months ago? Bunches of exoplanetary airships descended from the firmament, coming with alien technology, but also with wicked lies and collars of slavery. So the Evermind summoned all of her paladins and sent them out to reclaim the skylances of our ancestors, from before the Glass. She lit up the night with lances of atomic fire and hammers of holy tungsten, and tore the aliens to pieces.” The woman pauses to make a cogwheel symbol with both hands, a gesture she has to cut short to catch her tool before it falls over under its own weight. “Ah, but once they left, the goddess said that she would unleash a great dragon in the heavens to defend us from any other evil xenos; that only her chosen would be allowed to land. And so you must be chosen by the Evermind as worthy.” She nods emphatically at her own proclamation, finally snapping a few photographs from her wrist device. A dragon in the heavens... that KihaCorp drone ship? Could this Evermind, whatever that is, have hacked the V.I.-controlled ship and turned it on the Rushers coming through? It’s hard to tell where religious fervor ends and science begins with her, if she even understands the difference. “Who is this Evermind?” you ask. If there’s one thing a cultist can be relied on to do, it’s proselytize. “Our goddess! The only one of the ancient deities to have survived the Glass War, or at least to return to us again after the nukas and hyraxi nearly scoured the world with fire. She is the goddess of technology and wisdom, of progress and of innovation - but not too much! Mmm, we all suffered from unbridled techno-sorcery in the Glass War. Now the goddess has taught us wisdom; wisdom to learn from the past, and not repeat our mistakes. She is also the mistress of the Radwood, the last great bastion of untainted life on the planet, held safe beneath the Curtain of Light.” “The Radwood?” [rival.name] was heading for a forest before [rival.hisHer] ship was shot down; said that was where the probe was located. Sounds like your cousin got slapped down by the same atomic lances that the first Rushers did. “Is that somewhere I could go?”

The cultist gasps, nearly dropping her staff again. “Of course! A chosen must pay homage to the Evermind; you wish to meet the goddess! Well, you can’t fly there - the Curtain of Light protects her domain from anything that flies, be it airship or missiles - but there’s a great train that still runs from the nuka cities and through the Shield Mountains. Shineglass Station isn’t that far off; you can probably make it before you die of radtaint or get enslaved by hyraxi warbois.”
“Before I what?”

“Thanks for the pics!” the girl grins, tapping a few more buttons on her wrist device. “I have to run these back to a satlink before the radtaint fries my cogitator again. May the goddess’s wings shadow your steps, Emberian!”
She sketches a bow, drooling gold and white goo into a growing pool at her feet, before turning on a heel and dashing off with a mad giggle.

I mean, she could have melted you out of the sky with her skylances, or devoured you with her dragon of heaven, but she let you land! That means you must be cool too! C’moooon.” You scowl and take a step forward. “Fuck off.” She throws her hands up in exasperation. “Maybe the goddess needs her circuits rechecked with you. Jerk!” With a huff, she picks up her tool-staff and whirls her cloak around her, departing with a muttered series of curses on your motherboards. You keep watching until she vanishes over the horizon, making well sure that she’s gone before hopping down from the boarding ramp and rolling your shoulders. Alright, time to find your way towards that huge forest your cousin tried to land in. Even if it’s got anti-air that solid, maybe you can just walk in...

She follows your gaze, and titters. “Sorta! The Phaedran Railway Corporation, blessed be its name, left behind powerful defenses at its stations before the war. Look.” The lizard-woman grabs the shutter on the ticket kiosk and lifts it up, revealing a small, featureless white cubical inside, in which six humanoid armatures are standing statue-like, gazing at you with faintly glowing crimson eyes. Each of them has long tail with a wickedly-barbed mace head attachment, and you can see holstered energy pistols on their hips. “As long as everyone’s civil here, so are the holy guardians!” she says, lowering the shutter again. “Unfortunately that doesn’t apply to the wasteland, or even the electro-conveyance tracks connecting this station to the Radwood. All sorts of awful barbarians prey on the trains, and sometimes they’ll hit the station from afar by accident. But nobody is stupid enough to pick a fight here themselves! That’d be suicide.” “Uh-huh,” you say, glancing warily between her and the kiosk. “But the trains do still run?” She nods emphatically. “Yep yep! It’s basically the only way to get around. The Radwood’s a thousand kloms from here, y’know, not to mention on the other side of the Shield Mountains. That’s a pilgrimage that takes a lifetime! I’d never survive that walk by myself, but the divine conveyance lets me commute out here to tend to the poor machines beyond the Evermind’s direct touch.” Radwood... that’d be where [rival.name] tried to land. “We Sisters of the Volt have to bless and sanctify all technology, even if it’s too big or important to where it is for us to take it under the Curtain of Light for safekeeping. It’s our holy mission!” the lizard rambles on proudly, puffing out her prodigious chest. The flowering teats atop them release a particularly fragrant scent, mixing pleasantly with her incense. “Unfortunately, some are more obstinate than others. Like this little one here. All the prayers and rites I know can’t seem to assuage its grumpy spirit.” You look at what you assume is the ticket machine, with a monochrome CRT screen that’s currently reading: ++ERROR++ ++CODE 404: SERVER CONNECTION NOT FOUND++ ++PLEASE CONTACT AN EMPLOYEE FOR ASSISTANCE++ Beneath the error is a stylized emblem of a sleek electric train cresting a globe, underneath a crest proudly proclaiming “Phaedran Railway Corporation: Connecting the World Since 1725!” The lizard-girl pats the top of the machine like it’s a pouty child, and flicks some more incense-smoke at it. “Don’t worry little one. Your big sister Elcine will make it all better. Somehow. After all, I’m kinda stuck here until I do!” “Stuck here?” you ask. “Can you not just buy a ticket on the train?” “Eheh, yeah, that’s the problem!” She glances out onto the prairie waste, and the tracks stretching out to the horizon. “The whole system is a work of glorious divine automation... that doesn’t stop the train at any stations where nobody’s bought a ticket. So until this machine’s error is placated, the trains will never, ever stop at Shineglass Station ever again! At least not until somebody from Radwood buys a ticket to here but I’m the only Sister assigned out here, soooo that’ll probably be a while before somebody realizes I’m actually stranded and not just playing with the locals longer than I should again. Mmm, actually, High Sister Selphine diiiid kinda say she wasn’t gonna send anybody to come get me if I got distracted by hyraxi boys again, soooo maybe nobody’ll ever come!” “Oh, great.” You sigh. “So how do we fix this thing?” “With devoted prayer and the ritual of reconnection, of course!” The lizard — Elcine, you suppose — insists. “It will work... eventually! But perhaps the Emberian would like to try? I would love to see your kind’s technical skills in action, for surely one blessed by the Evermind must have brilliance beyond compare[var0 | | to make up for that attitude ].” ]

You lean against the kiosk next to Elcine and give you your flirtiest smile. “So, what do you say you and I get to know each other a little better?” Elcine blinks at you a couple times. “Hmm, okay! We can talk if you want, but [var0 |I really need to get back behind the Curtain, so I can’t go anywhere and miss the train. Maybe we could, y’know, get a little closer than just talking once we get to the end of the tracks? |I really gotta concentrate on fixing this machine and getting home. So maybe just talking for now, ‘kay? ]” You suppose that’ll have to do, for now.

“Tell me about your sisterhood?” “I am a Sister of the Volt, or a Voltaic Sister, if you prefer,” Elcine says proudly. “We are the handmaidens of the divine Evermind, she who rules and protects all of Phaedra. Our order was established in the aftermath of the great Glass War, to help our goddess recover, maintain, and safeguard what technology remains. It is by our holy work that there’s anything at all still working on this world — most importantly the glow-scrubbers in the remaining cities, and the agri-combines that keep them fed.” “So you’re a [var0 |cult |religion ]?” “Mmm, not quite. The Sisters are an arm of the Evermind’s faith, the same as the Knights Atomic or the Order of Our Martyred Wires. We were formed by her express order and given our mandate by her hand. So it’s less that we’re a faith in our own right, but we’ve got all our own snazzy uniforms and such!” Elcine clutches the hems of her loose-hanging robe and swishes this way and that, attempting to show off her swaggy attire but mostly just making all her plumpness jiggle and sway hypnotically. Her floral pecker swings around with her hips’ momentum, flecking moisture onto the platform. “We’re a pretty big order these days, too. The more things crumble out in the wastes, the more Sisters of the Volt are needed to keep everything running. There’s gotta be a couple hundred of us now, spread out all over the planet. Most of us at any one time are in the Evermind’s sanctuary, of course, but that’s the most holy and awesome place in the world so who’s complaining, right? Every machine is vital to the future of Phaedra, but none moreso than those that keep Her Divine Electrance in tip-top shape.” “What about machines used by the raiders, or us off-worlders? If my Codex broke, would you be obligated to fix it?” “It’s not the machines’ fault who uses them, or for what!” Elcine insists. “If we see a machine in distress, we aid it, no matter what.
